era_context: The late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires. The Ottoman Empire in decline; the Qing Dynasty at its height under Kangxi. Louis XIV dominates continental Europe. The War of the League of Augsburg is ongoing. The English succession crisis is building toward the Glorious Revolution (1688). Scientific revolution is in full swing with Newton's Principia published this year.

events.title: Isaac Newton publishes Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica (Principia)
events.description: Isaac Newton published his monumental work, the Principia Mathematica, which laid the foundations for classical mechanics and gravitation. The work presented Newton's laws of motion and universal gravitation, providing a mathematical framework for understanding the physical universe. The Principia was revolutionary in its scope and rigour, establishing the mathematical method as the foundation for natural philosophy. The work was published in Latin and initially had limited circulation among the educated elite, but it gradually became the foundation for the scientific revolution and the Enlightenment. The Principia's influence on subsequent scientific, philosophical, and intellectual development cannot be overstated.

sources.name: Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica (primary text, published 1687)

events.certainty_note: The Principia was published on 5 July 1687 (Julian calendar). The publication date is precisely documented.
cross_references: Part of broader scientific revolution (16th–18th centuries)

events.title: Battle of Mohács (Ottoman-Habsburg conflict)
events.description: The Battle of Mohács in 1687 was a significant Ottoman defeat by Habsburg forces under Charles of Lorraine. The battle was part of the Great Turkish War (1683–1699), in which the Ottoman Empire faced a coalition of European powers. The defeat at Mohács was a major blow to Ottoman prestige and military power, contributing to the broader Ottoman decline in the late 17th century. The battle resulted in significant territorial losses for the Ottomans and strengthened Habsburg control over Hungary. The battle is sometimes called the 'Second Battle of Mohács' to distinguish it from the famous 1526 battle.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Mohács is dated to 12 August 1687 (Julian calendar). The battle is well-documented in both Habsburg and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's edict on religion (Edict of Toleration)
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor issued an edict granting tolerance to Christianity and other religions in the Qing Empire. The edict allowed Christian missionaries to preach and establish churches, reflecting Kangxi's pragmatic approach to religion and his desire to attract Western learning and technology. The edict was part of Kangxi's broader policy of cultural integration and openness to foreign ideas. However, the tolerance was conditional and could be revoked; later tensions over religious practices (particularly the 'Chinese Rites Controversy') would lead to restrictions on Christian missionary activity. The edict represented a moment of relative openness in Qing religious policy.
key_figures: Ferdinand Verbiest (Jesuit missionary, deceased 1688, but his legacy relevant)

sources.name: Kangxi Emperor's edicts and court records

events.certainty_note: The edict is dated to 1687 in Qing records and is corroborated by Jesuit missionary accounts.
cross_references: Led to later tensions over Chinese Rites Controversy

events.title: Glorious Revolution: English succession crisis intensifies
events.description: In 1687, the English succession crisis intensified as King James II pursued policies that alarmed Protestant nobles and politicians. James II's promotion of Catholicism, his attempts to pack Parliament with Catholic sympathisers, and his apparent intention to establish a Catholic dynasty created a political crisis. In 1687, James II dissolved Parliament and ruled by prerogative, further alienating the political elite. The crisis would culminate in the Glorious Revolution in 1688, but 1687 saw the political situation deteriorate significantly. The year was marked by increasing polarisation between the king and the political establishment.
key_figures: William of Orange (potential invader, not yet committed)
events.certainty_note: The intensification of the succession crisis in 1687 is well-documented in contemporary sources. James II's dissolution of Parliament occurred in July 1687.
cross_references: Led to 1688-001 (Birth of James Francis Edward Stuart)

events.title: War of the League of Augsburg: Campaign in the Low Countries
events.description: The War of the League of Augsburg continued in 1687 with major campaigns in the Low Countries. French forces under Louis XIV's command pursued aggressive military operations against the coalition of European powers. The war was characterised by siege warfare and manoeuvre campaigns. In 1687, the French achieved some successes but faced increasing resistance from the allied coalition. The war would continue until 1697, when the Treaty of Ryswick would establish a temporary peace.
events.certainty_note: The 1687 campaigns in the War of the League of Augsburg are well-documented in military records.
cross_references: Part of 1688-004 (War of the League of Augsburg)

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian War (Morean War): Venetian advances
events.description: The Morean War (1684–1699) continued in 1687 with Venetian forces under Francesco Morosini achieving significant military successes against Ottoman forces. Venetian forces captured several Ottoman strongholds in the Peloponnese and the Aegean. The war was part of the broader decline of Ottoman power in the Mediterranean and southeastern Europe. The Venetian successes in 1687 were among the most significant achievements of the war, though the overall conflict would not be resolved until the Treaty of Karlowitz (1699).
events.certainty_note: The 1687 campaigns in the Morean War are well-documented in Venetian and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Aurangzeb's Mughal Empire at its territorial zenith
events.description: In 1687, the Mughal Empire under Emperor Aurangzeb reached its maximum territorial extent. Aurangzeb had spent decades conquering the Deccan sultanates and consolidating Mughal control over the Indian subcontinent. By 1687, the empire stretched from the Himalayas to the southern tip of the Deccan. However, this territorial expansion came at a cost: the empire was overextended, and Aurangzeb's religious policies (particularly his intolerance toward Hindus and his attempts to impose Islamic law) created internal tensions. The empire's zenith in 1687 would be followed by gradual decline as Aurangzeb aged and internal conflicts intensified.
events.certainty_note: The territorial extent of the Mughal Empire in 1687 is well-documented in Mughal records and European accounts. The empire's zenith is generally dated to this period.
cross_references: Led to 1707-001 (Aurangzeb's death and empire's decline)

events.title: Parthenon damaged by Venetian bombardment
events.description: During the Morean War, Venetian forces under Francesco Morosini bombarded Athens, which was held by Ottoman forces. A Venetian shell struck the Parthenon, which the Ottomans had been using as a powder magazine. The explosion caused catastrophic damage to the ancient Greek temple, destroying the roof and much of the interior structure. This event is one of the most significant instances of damage to the Parthenon in recorded history. The bombardment was a consequence of the broader Venetian-Ottoman conflict, but it had the unintended consequence of destroying one of the world's greatest architectural monuments. The damage to the Parthenon is a poignant example of the collateral damage of early modern warfare.
events.certainty_note: The bombardment of the Parthenon is dated to 1687 and is well-documented in contemporary sources. The damage to the Parthenon is visible in the structure itself.
cross_references: Part of broader history of the Parthenon

disconfirming_evidence: Newton's Principia is often portrayed as an immediate and universal triumph of the scientific revolution, but modern scholarship emphasises that the work was initially poorly understood, had limited circulation, and took decades to become the foundation of natural philosophy. Many of Newton's contemporaries and successors misunderstood or misapplied his ideas. Additionally, the Principia was not the only important scientific work of the period; other scientists and natural philosophers were pursuing different approaches and achieving significant results. The damage to the Parthenon is sometimes portrayed as a tragic loss of ancient Greek heritage, but modern scholarship emphasises that the Parthenon had already been significantly altered and damaged over the centuries, and that the 1687 bombardment, while destructive, was not the only or even the most significant damage to the structure.
historiographic_note: The year 1687 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly for England, France, the Low Countries, and the Mediterranean. Chinese sources are also rich for the Kangxi period. However, documentation is heavily weighted toward elite political, military, and intellectual events. The Mughal Empire is documented in both Mughal and European sources, but the European perspective is limited to coastal trading posts and may not accurately represent the broader empire. Non-elite perspectives, women's experiences, and the impact on common people are underrepresented across all regions. The scientific significance of Newton's Principia is well-documented, but the limited initial understanding and circulation of the work is sometimes overlooked in popular accounts.

graph_edges.note: Newton's Principia (1687) was followed by his Opticks (1704)
Battle of Mohács was part of Great Turkish War, leading to Treaty of Karlowitz
Succession crisis intensified, leading to birth of James Francis Edward Stuart
Succession crisis led to Glorious Revolution
Morean War campaigns led to bombardment of Parthenon
